label: delphi-r1-p3
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from enforcement realities: who would sue, what remedies exist, what actually happens in practice.
text: {"probability":70,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not a court finds at least partial — and probably \"substantial part\" — violation of art. XIII D, §6(b). The rate study's own admission that the $0.85 figure is derived \"in part\" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation closely tracks the defect condemned in Capistrano, and the 55%-to-rebates-for-non-payers spending pattern independently threatens §6(b)(2)'s use restriction and §6(b)(3)'s proportionality requirement. The 1.6x cost-differential finding gives the district a real, if partial, defense, making full validation or full invalidation less likely than a court severing/reducing the surcharge to the cost-justified increment and ordering refunds or fund reallocation for the excess — an outcome that itself satisfies \"in whole or substantial part\" as the proposition is framed.","rationales":["Capistrano holds that tiered/incremental water pricing must track the actual marginal cost of service at each tier, not conservation-incentive goals standing alone, and the district's own study language admitting a conservation-incentive component creates a direct, hard-to-explain-away doctrinal vulnerability on precisely that point.","Article XIII D, §6(b)(5) places the burden of proof on the agency to demonstrate compliance by a preponderance of the evidence, and a record containing express concessions (partial incentive-pricing derivation; rebate program benefiting non-payers) is a difficult evidentiary posture for the district to carry that burden.","Routing 55% of restricted-fund revenue into rebates that primarily benefit customers other than the surcharge-payers raises an independent, potentially outcome-determinative problem under §6(b)(2)'s 'no use other than the service for which imposed' rule and undercuts the proportionality nexus required by §6(b)(3), regardless of how the $0.85 figure itself is defended.","The 1.6x marginal-cost finding is a genuine, non-trivial cost-of-service anchor (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) that gives the district a colorable basis to defend at least a portion of the surcharge, making outright total invalidation somewhat less likely than a partial/severed remedy.","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil forecloses the district's fallback argument that consumption-based water charges are not property-related fees, so the district cannot escape Article XIII D scrutiny entirely and the case will be decided on cost-of-service/proportionality grounds rather than threshold applicability."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b) (paragraphs (1)-(5), including burden of proof)","Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926","Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586","Cal. Const. art. X, §2 (conservation mandate)","Water Code §375 (conservation rate authority)","Cal. Const. art. XIII C (Proposition 26 tax/fee distinction)","Gov. Code §66022 (validation/refund action procedure and limitations period)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether, numerically, the $0.85 surcharge falls within, at, or above the cost-justified differential implied by the 1.6x marginal-cost multiplier — the facts state the figure is only \"in part\" incentive-derived but don't quantify the gap, and that unstated magnitude determines whether a court's remedy is a minor trim (arguably not \"substantial\") or a major reduction/refund order (clearly \"substantial\"), which is the crux of the proposition's outcome."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not a court finds at least partial — and probably "substantial part" — violation of art. XIII D, §6(b). The rate study's own admission that the $0.85 figure is derived "in part" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation closely tracks the defect condemned in Capistrano, and the 55%-to-rebates-for-non-payers spending pattern independently threatens §6(b)(2)'s use restriction and §6(b)(3)'s proportionality requirement. The 1.6x cost-differential finding gives the district a real, if partial, defense, making full validation or full invalidation less likely than a court severing/reducing the surcharge to the cost-justified increment and ordering refunds or fund reallocation for the excess — an outcome that itself satisfies "in whole or substantial part" as the proposition is framed.
rationales: Capistrano holds that tiered/incremental water pricing must track the actual marginal cost of service at each tier, not conservation-incentive goals standing alone, and the district's own study language admitting a conservation-incentive component creates a direct, hard-to-explain-away doctrinal vulnerability on precisely that point.

Article XIII D, §6(b)(5) places the burden of proof on the agency to demonstrate compliance by a preponderance of the evidence, and a record containing express concessions (partial incentive-pricing derivation; rebate program benefiting non-payers) is a difficult evidentiary posture for the district to carry that burden.

Routing 55% of restricted-fund revenue into rebates that primarily benefit customers other than the surcharge-payers raises an independent, potentially outcome-determinative problem under §6(b)(2)'s 'no use other than the service for which imposed' rule and undercuts the proportionality nexus required by §6(b)(3), regardless of how the $0.85 figure itself is defended.

The 1.6x marginal-cost finding is a genuine, non-trivial cost-of-service anchor (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) that gives the district a colorable basis to defend at least a portion of the surcharge, making outright total invalidation somewhat less likely than a partial/severed remedy.

Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil forecloses the district's fallback argument that consumption-based water charges are not property-related fees, so the district cannot escape Article XIII D scrutiny entirely and the case will be decided on cost-of-service/proportionality grounds rather than threshold applicability.
authorities: Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b) (paragraphs (1)-(5), including burden of proof)
Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926
Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586
Cal. Const. art. XIII C (Proposition 26 tax/fee distinction)
Gov. Code §66022 (validation/refund action procedure and limitations period)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether, numerically, the $0.85 surcharge falls within, at, or above the cost-justified differential implied by the 1.6x marginal-cost multiplier — the facts state the figure is only "in part" incentive-derived but don't quantify the gap, and that unstated magnitude determines whether a court's remedy is a minor trim (arguably not "substantial") or a major reduction/refund order (clearly "substantial"), which is the crux of the proposition's outcome.
